text.title: Docker Layer Caching, Image Optimization, and Core Principles
text.summary: Layer caching strategy, base image selection, CMD vs ENTRYPOINT, image optimization techniques, networking, volume types, and anti-patterns.

rule.statement: Place dependency installation before source code COPY so dependency layers are cached across code changes.
rule.rationale: Docker invalidates cache for a layer and all subsequent layers when any input changes. Source code changes on every commit; dependency files change rarely. Copying dependencies first preserves the expensive install layer across most builds.

example.code: # Invalidates pip install cache on every code change COPY . . RUN pip install -r requirements.txt
# pip install cached; only invalidated when requirements.txt changes COPY requirements.txt . RUN pip install --no-cache-dir -r requirements.txt COPY . .

rule.statement: Use -slim for production balance; use Alpine or Distroless for maximum size reduction and security.
rule.rationale: Full images (python:3.12, ~900MB) include tools unnecessary in production and expand the attack surface. Distroless (~30MB) contains only the runtime and app; no shell means no interactive exploitation path.

list.item: Slim (python:3.12-slim, ~150MB): production balance — recommended starting point
Alpine (python:3.12-alpine, ~50MB): minimal size, musl libc (test compatibility)

rule.statement: Always use exec form (JSON array syntax) for CMD and ENTRYPOINT; never shell form in production.
rule.rationale: Shell form runs via /bin/sh -c, which becomes the PID 1 process. The shell does not forward SIGTERM to the child process, causing ungraceful shutdowns and missed graceful-drain windows in Kubernetes.

example.code: CMD python app.py # shell form — SIGTERM not forwarded

rule.statement: Use multi-stage builds to exclude build-time tooling from the runtime image.
rule.rationale: Build tools (compilers, test runners, dev dependencies) can be 10x the size of the runtime artifact. Multi-stage copies only the compiled binary or production packages into a minimal runtime image, reducing size 50-90% and eliminating build tools from the attack surface.

list.item: Named volume (mydata:/app/data): persistent data, managed by Docker, survives container removal
Bind mount (./src:/app/src): development use, source code or config files from host
tmpfs (--tmpfs /tmp): temporary data, sensitive in-memory data, not persisted to disk

antipattern.description: Using the latest tag for base images or pushed images.
antipattern.reason: latest is mutable — a base image update can change behavior without any change to the Dockerfile. Pin specific versions (python:3.12.7-slim) for reproducible builds.
Root inside container maps to root on host if namespace isolation is bypassed. Create a non-root user in the Dockerfile and set USER before the final CMD.

antipattern.description: Storing secrets in ENV instructions or build ARGs.
antipattern.reason: ENV values are visible in docker inspect, image history, and any process that reads /proc/self/environ. Use Docker secrets, runtime environment injection, or an external vault at container startup.

Without HEALTHCHECK, orchestrators (Kubernetes, ECS, Swarm) cannot distinguish a running-but-broken container from a healthy one. Containers stuck in crash-loops with no readiness signals cause silent traffic loss.
